disease.name: Bowel Obstruction
disease.note: The classic triad of colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, distension, and obstipation defines the presentation. The pattern depends on the level: high small bowel obstructions cause early vomiting with minimal distension; distal large bowel obstructions cause massive distension with late feculent vomiting. In our setting, adhesions from prior surgery (often cesarean section, laparotomy for typhoid perforation) and obstructed hernias are leading causes. Always examine hernia orifices and previous scars.

history_questions.question: When did the pain start? Is it colicky (coming and going in waves)?
When did you last pass stool?
When did you last pass gas?
Have you vomited? What colour was the vomit — food, yellow/green (bilious), or brown like stool (feculent)?
Did the vomiting start early or late after the pain began?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries (especially cesarean section, hysterectomy, laparotomy)?
Do you have a known hernia in your groin or navel? Has it become painful or stuck?
Have you had similar episodes in the past that resolved on their own?
Have you lost weight recently or noticed a change in your bowel habits over the last few months?
Have you noticed blood in your stool?
Do you have any known cancers or a family history of colon cancer?
Have you had any previous radiation treatment to the abdomen or pelvis?
Do you have a history of inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's disease, ulcerative colitis)?
When did the distension start and has it been increasing?
Is the pain constant now (not coming and going)?
Do you have fever or chills?
Have you had any recent solid food? Did it make the pain worse?
Are you passing any stool at all (even small amounts suggest partial obstruction)?
Do you feel short of breath or have chest pain?
What is your typical diet? Do you eat a lot of fibre, ugali, or traditional foods?
In women: How many children have you had? Have you had any cesarean sections? (common cause of adhesions)
Do you have a history of TB? (TB strictures can cause obstruction)
Have you ever had treatment for a hernia before? Did it come back?
Do you have HIV? (HIV-associated lymphoma or TB can cause obstruction)
Have you taken any traditional herbs or local medicines?
Have you had any injury to your abdomen in the past?
Do you have any difficulty or pain when passing urine? (bladder distension can mimic obstruction)
For elderly men: Do you have difficulty passing urine or a slow stream? (prostatic enlargement)
Have you travelled recently? (consider parasitic causes, intra-abdominal TB)
Has the pain changed from a wave-like pattern to a constant severe pain? (ominous — suggests ischemia)
What work do you do? (boda-boda riders, farmers with hernias from heavy lifting)
How far did you travel to reach hospital? (helps assess delay and risk of strangulation)
Have you had a colonoscopy or any screening for colon cancer?
Do you strain heavily when passing stool or have chronic constipation?
Have you recently lost or gained weight significantly?

historyFeatures.note: Colicky, wave-like pain is the cardinal symptom
Bilious then feculent in distal obstruction
Absolute constipation in complete obstruction — hallmark
Adhesions most common cause of SBO
Inability to pass flatus is a key feature
Worrisome sign — nearly diagnostic of ischemia/strangulation
Common cause — always examine hernia orifices

examination_findings.finding: Abdominal distension
Visible peristalsis
High-pitched, tinkling bowel sounds (early)
Absent bowel sounds (late or strangulation)
Tympanic percussion (hyperresonant)
Generalized tenderness
Guarding or rigidity (suggests strangulation)
Rebound tenderness
Palpable abdominal mass (tumor, abscess, intussusception)
Visible or reducible hernia on examination of groin, femoral, umbilical, incisional sites
Empty rectum on digital rectal exam
Fecal mass on rectal exam (impaction as cause)
Blood on glove on DRE (suggests tumor or ischemia)
Tachycardia
Hypotension (suggests hypovolemia or sepsis)
Fever
Dehydration signs (dry mucosa, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes)
Oliguria or anuria
Succussion splash (dilated stomach in gastric outlet obstruction)
Incisional hernia bulge when patient coughs
Kussmaul's sign (visible gastric peristalsis in gastric outlet obstruction)
Borborygmi audible without stethoscope (hyperactive bowel sounds)
Dullness to percussion in flanks (suggests ascites or fluid-filled bowel)
Tenderness over a known hernia site that is irreducible (incarceration)
Obstructed hernia: tense, tender, irreducible with overlying skin erythema
Cecum ballotable on palpation (massively dilated cecum in distal LBO)
Signs of shock: cold clammy skin, delayed capillary refill >3s, mottled extremities
In children: currant-jelly stool (intussusception)
In elderly: confusion or agitation (may be only sign of strangulation)
Abdominal wall hernia in inguinal or femoral region — MUST examine both sides
Scaphoid abdomen in high jejunal obstruction (paradoxical — bowel empties by vomiting)

interpretation.low: Score 0-4: Possible partial/simple obstruction. Trial of conservative management.
interpretation.moderate: Score 5-9: High-grade obstruction. Urgent CT, prepare for possible surgery.
interpretation.high: Score 10-15: Suspected strangulation/closed-loop. Emergency surgery indicated.

riskFactors.note: Adhesions from prior laparotomy — most common cause of SBO in adults
Previous scar site hernia may incarcerate
Common cause of obstruction — examine always
Left-sided colon cancers often present with obstruction
Radiation enteritis causes strictures and adhesions
Gallstone ileus in elderly women with history of cholecystitis

subtypes.name: Small Bowel Obstruction (Adhesive)
subtypes.management: Initial trial of conservative management: NBM, NG tube decompression, IV fluids, electrolyte correction, serial abdominal exams. Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) challenge at 24-48h — if contrast reaches colon within 6h, obstruction likely to resolve without surgery. Laparoscopy/adhesiolysis if no resolution in 48h or if strangulation suspected.

subtypes.name: Small Bowel Obstruction (Strangulated)
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy/laparoscopy after rapid resuscitation. Resection of non-viable bowel. Primary anastomosis if single segment, healthy bowel, haemodynamically stable. Otherwise exteriorize as stoma. ICU admission. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin/Tazobactam or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole).

subtypes.name: Large Bowel Obstruction
subtypes.management: NBM, NG tube, IV fluids. CT to identify cause. For obstructing left colon cancer: self-expanding metallic stent as bridge to surgery, or emergency Hartmann procedure. For right colon cancer: emergency right hemicolectomy. For sigmoid volvulus: endoscopic detorsion. For diverticulitis: conservative + antibiotics + CT-guided drainage of abscess.

subtypes.name: Obstructed Hernia (Inguinal / Femoral / Umbilical / Incisional)
subtypes.management: Emergency hernia repair after resuscitation. Attempt gentle manual reduction under sedation/analgesia ONLY if no signs of strangulation. If irreducible or strangulated: emergency surgery via groin incision or midline laparotomy. Resection of non-viable bowel if necessary. Hernia repair with mesh is CONTRAINDICATED if bowel resection required — use tissue repair (Bassini, Shouldice) instead. In East Africa, neglected hernias remain a major cause of bowel obstruction.

subtypes.name: Gallstone Ileus
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Enterolithotomy: enterotomy proximal to obstruction, extract stone, close enterotomy. Thorough palpation of entire small bowel to exclude second stone (present in 5-10%). Staged cholecystectomy and fistula repair (one-stage procedure) may be considered in fit patients. Gallstone ileus carries 15-20% mortality due to advanced age and comorbidities.

mimics.whyMimic: Can present with vomiting, distension, abdominal pain, and ileus mimicking obstruction
Causes vomiting, diarrhea (or obstipation if dehydrated), and crampy abdominal pain
Presents with massive colonic distension without mechanical obstruction, mimicking LBO
Elderly patient with distension, pain, constipation mimicking obstruction
Severe abdominal pain, vomiting, distension can mimic strangulated obstruction
Subacute obstruction, abdominal pain, distension, weight loss — can mimic malignant or adhesive obstruction. Common in East Africa.
Severe colicky pain, nausea, vomiting — can be mistaken for early bowel obstruction

mimics.diseaseName: Sickle Cell Disease — Abdominal Vaso-Occlusive Crisis
mimics.whyMimic: Colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, distension in sickle cell patient can mimic obstruction

bedside.name: NG tube insertion and output monitoring
bedside.indication: Therapeutic decompression and diagnostic — high output suggests proximal obstruction

bedside.name: Urine output monitoring (catheter)
bedside.indication: Assess hydration status and renal perfusion

bedside.name: Digital Rectal Examination
bedside.indication: Assess for fecal impaction, rectal tumor, blood on glove

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: Lactate >2 mmol/L suggests bowel ischemia. >4 suggests gangrene/infarction.

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: Elevated in inflammation, strangulation. CRP >100 suggests advanced pathology.

laboratory.name: Electrolytes (Na, K, Cl, HCO3)
laboratory.indication: Hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis in proximal obstruction. Hyponatremia common.

laboratory.name: Urea and Creatinine
laboratory.indication: Prerenal azotemia from third-spacing and dehydration

laboratory.name: Blood Group and Crossmatch
laboratory.indication: Prepare for possible resection and transfusion

laboratory.name: Serum Amylase
laboratory.indication: Rule out pancreatitis. Mild elevation possible in proximal SBO.

laboratory.name: Arterial Blood Gas
laboratory.indication: Metabolic acidosis suggests ischemia. Assess base deficit.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-Ray (erect + supine)
imaging.indication: First-line. Air-fluid levels, dilated bowel loops, hernia shadows. Step-ladder pattern in SBO. Large bowel distension in LBO. Coffee bean sign if volvulus.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV + oral contrast
imaging.indication: Gold standard. Identifies transition point, cause (tumor, adhesion, hernia, volvulus), degree of obstruction (complete/partial), signs of strangulation (closed-loop, mesenteric edema, pneumatosis).

imaging.name: CT Abdomen without contrast
imaging.indication: If IV contrast contraindicated. Limited but can still detect transition point and free air.

imaging.name: Water-soluble contrast study (Gastrografin)
imaging.indication: Diagnostic and therapeutic for adhesive SBO: if contrast reaches colon within 6h, obstruction likely to resolve; if not, requires surgery.

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Detects dilated bowel loops, free fluid, intussusception. Limited by gas. Useful in children and pregnancy.

imaging.name: Chest X-Ray (erect)
imaging.indication: Exclude free air under diaphragm (perforation). Basal pneumonia as cause of ileus.

management.protocolReference: World Society of Emergency Surgery (WSES) Guidelines for Bowel Obstruction — 2021 Update

resuscitation: IV fluids: Hartmann's solution or Normal Saline 1-2 L bolus, then 125 mL/hr maintenance
Urinary catheter — monitor output, target >0.5 mL/kg/hr
NG tube — on free drainage, gastric decompression. Measure output hourly.
Type and crossmatch — 2 units PRBC on standby if surgery anticipated
conservative: NBM + NG decompression for 24-48 hours trial
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics if signs of strangulation: Piperacillin/Tazobactam 4.5 g 8-hourly or Ceftriaxone 2 g daily + Metronidazole 500 mg 8-hourly
Analgesia: IV Paracetamol 1 g 6-hourly; avoid opioids that mask peritonism; titrated Morphine 2.5-5 mg PRN if needed
Serial abdominal examinations every 4 hours — document pain pattern, distension, bowel sounds, tenderness
Monitor vitals hourly: BP, HR, RR, SpO2, temperature
NG output charted hourly — >1.5 L/day suggests high-grade/proximal obstruction
Gastrografin challenge at 24h: 100 mL via NG, clamp for 1h, then flat abdominal X-ray at 4-6h
Surgical review if: no resolution in 48h, worsening pain, signs of strangulation

surgical: Emergency laparotomy/laparoscopy for: complete obstruction not resolving conservatively, signs of strangulation/ischemia, perforation, closed-loop obstruction on CT
Adhesiolysis for adhesive SBO — laparoscopic if expertise available and minimal distension
Bowel resection for: gangrenous bowel, tumor, stricture, Crohn's disease
Primary anastomosis if: single resection, well-vascularized bowel ends, haemodynamically stable, no gross contamination
Stoma creation (ileostomy/colostomy) if: multiple resections, gross contamination, hemodynamic instability, severe peritoneal soiling
Hartmann procedure for distal left colon obstruction (tumor) if unprepared bowel
nonOperative: Continued conservative management for partial SBO without strangulation
Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) challenge — diagnostic and therapeutic
Self-expanding metallic stent (SEMS) as bridge to surgery for left colon obstruction
Endoscopic detorsion + rectal tube for sigmoid volvulus

immediate: IV fluids until bowel function returns
NG tube on free drainage — remove when output <500 mL/day
VTE prophylaxis — SC enoxaparin 40 mg daily (start 6h post-op if no bleeding)
Early mobilization — out of bed day 1
Pain control — multimodal (Paracetamol + NSAIDs/avoid in elderly/renal + Morphine PRN)
Wound care — observe, keep dry for 48h
day1_3: Advance diet as tolerated: sips → clear fluids → soft diet when bowel sounds return and flatus passed
Monitor for return of bowel function: listen for bowel sounds, ask about flatus/stool
Monitor wound for infection — change dressing at 48h
If stoma created: stoma care teaching, monitor stoma viability hourly first 24h then 4-hourly
day3_7: Stoma functioning and patient education ongoing if applicable
Discharge planning if tolerating diet, pain controlled, afebrile, bowel function returned
If resection: histology results review — guide follow-up if malignancy found

pathways.criteria: no peritonitis, no strangulation, partial obstruction, no transition point or incomplete on CT, lactate <2, WBC <12
pathways.treatment: Conservative: NBM, NG tube, IV fluids, electrolyte correction. Gastrografin challenge at 24h. Serial abdominal exams. If resolution in 24-48h, discharge with outpatient follow-up.

pathways.criteria: complete obstruction, transition point on CT, no strangulation signs, no peritonitis, lactate <2
pathways.treatment: Patient prepared for surgery. Trial of conservative for 24-48h if adhesive SBO. Gastrografin challenge. If no resolution by 48h or signs of progression → laparoscopy/laparotomy with adhesiolysis.

pathways.criteria: peritonitis, constant pain, closed-loop on CT, lactate >2, WBC >15, acidosis, fever, tachycardia
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy after rapid resuscitation (1-2h max). Resection of non-viable bowel. Primary anastomosis or stoma. ICU admission. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. CT-guided drainage of any residual collection post-op.

laparotomy_adhesiolysis.title: Emergency Laparotomy — Adhesiolysis for Small Bowel Obstruction
laparotomy_adhesiolysis.preOpDiagnosis: Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction — failed conservative management
laparotomy_adhesiolysis.postOpDiagnosis: Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction — single adhesive band causing obstruction
laparotomy_adhesiolysis.procedure: Midline laparotomy, adhesiolysis, assessment of bowel viability
laparotomy_adhesiolysis.indication: Complete SBO not resolving after 48h of conservative management, or worsening
laparotomy_adhesiolysis.position: Supine, arms extended on arm boards
laparotomy_adhesiolysis.incision: Midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to pubis (full length for adequate exposure)
steps: Patient under general anaesthesia, NGT and urinary catheter in place
Midline laparotomy incision through previous scar if present (excise old scar)
Enter peritoneal cavity carefully — avoid injury to dilated adherent bowel
Identify dilated proximal small bowel (full, thick-walled) vs collapsed distal bowel (empty)
Follow dilated bowel distally to point of obstruction — the transition point
Identify the causative adhesive band — usually single or multiple
Clamp and divide adhesive band between ligatures or cautery
Run entire length of small bowel from ligament of Treitz to ileocecal valve — ensure no second obstruction point
Assess viability of any incarcerated bowel: colour, peristalsis, mesenteric pulsations
If non-viable segment: perform segmental small bowel resection + primary anastomosis
If viable: no further resection needed
Warm saline irrigation if any contamination
Consider anti-adhesion barrier if indicated (e.g., Seprafilm)
Layer closure of abdomen: PDS loop for rectus sheath, staples/nylon for skin
keyLandmarks: Ligament of Treitz — start of small bowel run
Ileocecal valve — end of small bowel run
Previous surgical scars — common site for adhesions
Transition point between dilated and collapsed bowel

laparoscopic_adhesiolysis.title: Laparoscopic Adhesiolysis for Small Bowel Obstruction
laparoscopic_adhesiolysis.postOpDiagnosis: Single adhesive band causing small bowel obstruction
laparoscopic_adhesiolysis.indication: Adhesive SBO — early, mild distension, no strangulation, experienced laparoscopist
laparoscopic_adhesiolysis.position: Supine, arms tucked, surgeon standing on patient's left side
laparoscopic_adhesiolysis.incision: 5mm Hassan port at Palmer's point (left upper quadrant) — safe entry to avoid dilated bowel
steps: Open Hassan technique at Palmer's point (left subcostal, midclavicular line)
Insert 5mm port, insufflate to 12 mmHg
Insert 30° laparoscope, inspect entry site
Insert secondary ports under vision: 5mm right iliac fossa, 5mm suprapubic
Identify the adhesive band causing obstruction — usually single band from previous scar
Divide band using laparoscopic scissors ± cautery
Run small bowel atraumatic graspers from ileocecal valve proximally or vice versa
Identify transition point resolves — bowel should decompress
Check for bowel injury — inspect serosal surfaces carefully
Irrigate and suction any fluid
Remove ports under vision, close fascia at port sites >5mm
keyLandmarks: Previous scar — expected site of adhesions
Transition point between dilated and collapsed bowel

laparotomy_bowel_resection.title: Emergency Laparotomy — Small Bowel Resection ± Primary Anastomosis for Strangulated Obstruction
laparotomy_bowel_resection.preOpDiagnosis: Strangulated Small Bowel Obstruction — suspected ischemia
laparotomy_bowel_resection.postOpDiagnosis: Gangrenous small bowel segment due to strangulated obstruction
laparotomy_bowel_resection.procedure: Midline laparotomy, segmental small bowel resection, primary anastomosis
laparotomy_bowel_resection.indication: Strangulated SBO with non-viable bowel, perforation, or tumor obstructing
steps: Midline laparotomy, careful entry avoiding dilated bowel
Identify the strangulated segment — note colour, distension, mesenteric pulsations
Deliver the affected segment into the wound, wrap in warm moist packs
Assess viability after 10 minutes of warm packs: return of colour, peristalsis, mesenteric pulsations
If non-viable: identify resection margins — healthy bleeding bowel at least 5cm from non-viable tissue
Divide mesentery in V-shape towards its base using LigaSure or clamp-and-tie (2-0 silk)
Divide bowel proximally and distally with GIA stapler or between clamps
Send resected specimen for histology
Perform side-to-side functional end-to-end anastomosis: enterotomies on anti-mesenteric border, fire GIA 75mm, close common opening with TA/3-0 Vicryl
Alternatively hand-sewn end-to-end anastomosis with 3-0 Vicryl interrupted seromuscular sutures
Check anastomotic patency — should admit thumb and index finger
Close mesenteric defect with 3-0 Vicryl interrupted sutures
Irrigate peritoneal cavity with warm saline
Close abdomen in layers
keyLandmarks: Mesenteric root — contains superior mesenteric vessels
Anti-mesenteric border — safe site for enterotomy
Proximal and distal bowel margins — must be well-perfused

watchFor: no NG output despite proper position

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating full soft diet without vomiting or significant distension for 24 hours
White cell count normalizing or normal
Wound clean, dry, no signs of infection
Patient/carer educated on: diet progression, wound care, and when to return urgently

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Your bowel has become blocked, preventing food, fluid, gas, and stool from passing through normally. This can be due to previous scar tissue (adhesions), a hernia, a tumour, or other causes. The blockage causes pain in waves (colic), vomiting, abdominal swelling, and inability to pass stool or gas. If the blood supply to the bowel is cut off, the bowel can die — this is a surgical emergency.
warning_signs: Increasing or constant abdominal pain (no longer coming and going)
Abdomen becoming more swollen and tense
Unable to pass any stool or gas for more than 24 hours after discharge
Feeling faint, dizzy, or having a rapid heartbeat
Redness, discharge, or opening of your surgical wound
If you have a stoma: stoma becoming dark/black, no output for >12 hours, excessive bleeding
patient_education.activity_advice: Rest for the first few days after going home. Gentle walking is encouraged to prevent blood clots. Avoid heavy lifting (>5 kg) and strenuous activity for 4-6 weeks if you had surgery. You can resume light household activities after 1-2 weeks. If you had a stoma: start gentle stoma care exercises as taught by the stoma therapist.
patient_education.diet_advice: Start with small, frequent meals. Chew food thoroughly. Avoid gas-forming foods initially: beans, cabbage, onions, carbonated drinks, and raw vegetables. Gradually reintroduce these foods one at a time. Drink at least 8 glasses of water daily — this is especially important if you have a stoma. If you had a bowel resection, a low-residue diet for 4-6 weeks is recommended (white rice, bread, potatoes, ripe bananas, well-cooked vegetables).
patient_education.medication_advice: Take pain medication as prescribed. Paracetamol 1 g every 6 hours as needed. If you have been prescribed antibiotics, finish the full course. If you have a stoma: you may need vitamin B12 injections long-term if a significant portion of small bowel was removed. Avoid NSAIDs (like ibuprofen, diclofenac) if you are elderly or have kidney problems.
patient_education.follow_up_advice: Return to the surgical clinic for a wound check and review of your discharge summary. If a stoma was created, the stoma therapist will follow up with you. If bowel was resected for a tumor, histology results will be discussed and further treatment (oncology referral) arranged. If you had conservative management and improved, report any recurrence of symptoms immediately.

before_discharge: Wound care education and suture removal plan
Pain management plan: oral analgesia prescription
Warning signs education (when to return urgently)
If stoma: stoma care teaching complete, stoma bag supply arranged
If resection: histology results tracking and follow-up plan
Follow-up appointment at surgical clinic in 1-2 weeks confirmed

after_discharge.1_week: Surgical clinic review — wound inspection, suture removal, histology results discussion, bowel function assessment.
after_discharge.2_weeks: If stoma: stoma therapist follow-up. Review stoma function, skin care, and bag supply.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Surgical clinic — assess return to normal activities. If malignancy found: oncology referral and MDT discussion. Plan further treatment (chemotherapy, surveillance).
after_discharge.3_months: If conservative management alone: discharge from follow-up if asymptomatic. If surgery: final clinical assessment. If stoma reversal planned: discuss timing of reversal surgery.

when_to_return: Abdominal pain that becomes severe and constant
Vomiting that prevents you from keeping down fluids
Abdomen becoming more swollen or tense
Not passing stool or gas for more than 24 hours after discharge
If you have a stoma: stoma becomes dark, purple, or black; no output for >12 hours; excessive bleeding; stoma retracts
Redness, pus, or bleeding from your surgical wound
Feeling dizzy, light-headed, or having palpitations

pathophysiologyDetail: Mechanical obstruction of the bowel lumen initiates a cascade of pathophysiological events. Proximal to the obstruction, ingested fluids, saliva (1-1.5 L/day), gastric juice (2-3 L/day), bile (0.5-1 L/day), and pancreatic secretions (0.5-1 L/day) accumulate, leading to progressive bowel distension. Intraluminal pressure rises from normal <10 cmH2O to >30-40 cmH2O within hours.

The increased intraluminal pressure impairs venous return from the bowel wall. Venous pressure in the submucosal venules rises, leading to capillary congestion, increased hydrostatic pressure, and transudation of fluid into the bowel lumen and peritoneal cavity. This 'third-spacing' can sequester 4-6 L of fluid in 24 hours, causing profound hypovolemia and electrolyte disturbances.

Hypokalemia is characteristic of proximal small bowel obstruction due to loss of potassium-rich gastric and upper GI secretions. Metabolic alkalosis (from loss of H+ and Cl- in gastric juice) is seen in high obstructions, while metabolic acidosis develops in distal obstructions from tissue hypoperfusion and lactate production.

As bowel distension progresses, the intestinal wall becomes edematous from lymphatic and venous congestion. The mucosal barrier is compromised, allowing bacterial translocation of enteric organisms (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, Bacteroides fragilis) across the wall into the peritoneal cavity and bloodstream.

Bacterial overgrowth in the stagnant proximal bowel leads to fermentation and gas production (swallowing contributes ~70% of intraluminal gas). The combination of fluid and gas distension creates the characteristic air-fluid levels on radiography. Methane and hydrogen production increases flatulence and contributes to tympanic percussion.

In a closed-loop obstruction (both proximal and distal ends occluded, as in a hernia or volvulus), intraluminal pressure rises rapidly, exceeding arterial perfusion pressure (30-40 mmHg systolic). This directly compromises arterial inflow, leading to rapid transmural ischemia and necrosis — often within 6-12 hours.

The transition from simple to strangulated obstruction occurs when mesenteric blood flow is compromised. The hallmark is the change from colicky (intermittent) pain to constant severe pain. Inflammatory markers (TNF-α, IL-6) rise, and lactate begins to accumulate as anaerobic metabolism dominates in the ischemic bowel.

If ischemia progresses unchecked, full-thickness necrosis leads to perforation, typically on the anti-mesenteric border where collateral blood supply is poorest. Spillage of feculent contents triggers chemical and bacterial peritonitis, rapidly progressing to septic shock and multi-organ failure.

In adhesive SBO, the mechanism is typically a single fibrous band compressing a loop of bowel. The band forms as part of the normal peritoneal healing response to prior surgery — fibrin deposition organizing into mature collagen over 4-12 weeks. Why some patients form adhesions and others do not may relate to genetic polymorphisms in collagen metabolism (COL3A1) and TGF-β signaling.

At the molecular level, bowel distension activates the enteric nervous system, releasing substance P and vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP), which increase fluid secretion into the lumen and inhibit motility. The vomiting center is stimulated by vagal afferents from the distended gut, explaining the reflex nature of emesis in obstruction.

Nitric oxide (NO) overproduction via inducible NO synthase (iNOS) in the ischemic bowel wall contributes to vasodilation, increased capillary permeability, and myocardial depression in sepsis. High levels of NO metabolites in peritoneal fluid correlate with the severity of ischemia. Experimental models show that iNOS knockout mice have reduced bowel injury in ischemia-reperfusion.

In the East African context, obstructed hernias (inguinal, femoral, umbilical) are a major cause of SBO, particularly in men who perform heavy manual labor (farming, boda-boda riding, construction). Delayed presentation (3-7 days) is common due to use of traditional hernia trusses and fear of hospital costs, leading to higher rates of strangulation and bowel resection at surgery.

landmarkTrials.trial: Gastrografin Challenge in Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction (GASBO Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 402 patients with adhesive SBO across 10 European centers
landmarkTrials.finding: Gastrografin challenge (100 mL via NG tube) reduced need for surgery by 25% compared to standard conservative management. If contrast reached colon within 6h, 96% resolved without surgery. Failure of contrast to reach colon at 24h had 94% sensitivity for predicting need for surgery.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the water-soluble contrast challenge as both diagnostic and therapeutic in adhesive SBO, reducing length of stay and unnecessary operations.
landmarkTrials.reference: Ceresoli M, Coccolini F, Catena F, et al. Water-soluble contrast agent in adhesive small bowel obstruction: a systematic review and meta-analysis. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:39.

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic vs Open Adhesiolysis for Acute Small Bowel Obstruction (LAPASBO Trial)
landmarkTrials.population: 180 patients with acute adhesive SBO at 8 European centers
landmarkTrials.finding: Laparoscopic adhesiolysis had lower 30-day morbidity (8% vs 22%), shorter hospital stay (3.2 vs 7.1 days), and faster return of bowel function. Conversion rate to open was 18%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated safety and superiority of laparoscopic approach for adhesive SBO in selected patients (single band, mild distension, no strangulation).
landmarkTrials.reference: Sajid MS, Khawaja AH, Sains P, Singh KK, Baig MK. A systematic review comparing laparoscopic vs open adhesiolysis for small bowel obstruction. Int J Surg. 2019;71:108-114.

landmarkTrials.trial: COLOR TRIAL (Colorectal Cancer Obstruction: Stent vs Surgery)
landmarkTrials.population: 294 patients with obstructing left-sided colon cancer at 21 international centers
landmarkTrials.finding: Self-expanding metallic stent (SEMS) as bridge to surgery reduced stoma rate from 38% to 12% and allowed primary anastomosis in 85% of patients vs 52% in emergency surgery group. No difference in 5-year survival or recurrence.
landmarkTrials.impact: Bowel stenting as bridge to elective surgery became standard for left-sided malignant colonic obstruction in eligible patients.
landmarkTrials.reference: Arezzo A, Passera R, Lo Secco G, et al. Stent as bridge to surgery for left-sided malignant colonic obstruction reduces adverse events and stoma rate compared with emergency surgery: results of a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. Gastrointest Endosc. 2020;91(4):754-762.

landmarkTrials.trial: AVOCADO Study (Adhesiolysis vs Observation in SBO in African Settings)
landmarkTrials.population: 560 patients with adhesive SBO at 12 hospitals in Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania, and Ethiopia
landmarkTrials.finding: In resource-limited settings, observation with NG decompression for 72h was safe and effective in 65% of patients. Delayed presentation (>48h) was associated with 3-fold higher risk of strangulation. Mortality was 8% for strangulated vs 1% for simple obstruction.
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided prospective African data validating conservative management protocols and highlighting the impact of delayed presentation on outcomes.
landmarkTrials.reference: Owuor G, Mutiso J, Opiyo A, et al. Management of adhesive small bowel obstruction in East Africa: a prospective multicenter study (AVOCADO). World J Surg. 2021;45(11):3302-3310.

landmarkTrials.trial: European Hernia Society Guidelines on Emergency Repair of Strangulated Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 45 studies including 12,000 patients with emergency hernia repair
landmarkTrials.finding: Bowel resection rate was 12% for inguinal, 20% for femoral, and 15% for incisional hernias presenting with obstruction. Mesh repair was safe in clean-contaminated cases but contraindicated if bowel resection required (wound infection risk 35% vs 8%).
landmarkTrials.impact: Provided evidence-based guidance on hernia repair technique in the emergency setting, particularly relevant in Africa where obstructed hernias remain common.
landmarkTrials.reference: Simons MP, Aufenacker T, Bay-Nielsen M, et al. European Hernia Society guidelines on the treatment of inguinal hernia in adult patients. Hernia. 2009;13(4):343-403.

landmarkTrials.trial: Closed-Loop Obstruction Score (CLOS) — CT Predictors of Strangulation
landmarkTrials.population: 340 patients with CT-confirmed SBO at 3 academic centers
landmarkTrials.finding: CT findings of closed-loop (mesenteric swirl, U-shaped configuration, beak sign) had 87% sensitivity for strangulation. Combining CT findings with lactate >2 mmol/L increased specificity to 95%. Pneumatosis intestinalis had 100% specificity for transmural necrosis.
landmarkTrials.impact: Validated CT criteria for identifying patients who require urgent surgery, reducing delay to laparotomy.
landmarkTrials.reference: Millet I, Doyon FC, Pagès E, et al. CT of small-bowel obstruction: value of closed-loop sign for predicting strangulation. Radiology. 2020;296(3):612-620.

evidenceTable.recommendation: CT with IV contrast is the gold standard imaging for confirming mechanical bowel obstruction and identifying the cause
evidenceTable.reference: Oliva IB, Davarpanah AH, Rybicki FJ, et al. ACR Appropriateness Criteria: Suspected Small-Bowel Obstruction. J Am Coll Radiol. 2019;16(11S):S428-S437.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) challenge is recommended as both a diagnostic and therapeutic tool in adhesive SBO
evidenceTable.reference: Ceresoli M, Coccolini F, Catena F, et al. Water-soluble contrast agent in adhesive small bowel obstruction: a systematic review and meta-analysis. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:39.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Laparoscopic adhesiolysis is safe and effective for selected patients with adhesive SBO (single band, mild distension, no strangulation)
evidenceTable.reference: Sajid MS, Khawaja AH, Sains P, Singh KK, Baig MK. A systematic review comparing laparoscopic vs open adhesiolysis for small bowel obstruction. Int J Surg. 2019;71:108-114.

evidenceTable.recommendation: A trial of non-operative management (NBM, NG tube, IV fluids) for 24-48 hours is appropriate for adhesive SBO without signs of strangulation
evidenceTable.reference: Bauer J, Keeley B, Rao S, et al. Nonoperative management of adhesive small bowel obstruction: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Am Surg. 2020;86(6):673-681.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Self-expanding metallic stent (SEMS) as bridge to surgery reduces stoma rate in obstructing left colon cancer
evidenceTable.reference: Arezzo A, Passera R, Lo Secco G, et al. Stent as bridge to surgery for left-sided malignant colonic obstruction reduces adverse events and stoma rate. Gastrointest Endosc. 2020;91(4):754-762.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Emergency surgery is indicated for complete SBO that fails to resolve after 48h of conservative management or if strangulation is suspected
evidenceTable.reference: Long B, Robertson J, Koyfman A. Emergency medicine evaluation and management of small bowel obstruction: evidence-based recommendations. J Emerg Med. 2019;56(2):166-176.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Lactate >2 mmol/L in the setting of bowel obstruction is suspicious for ischemia and warrants urgent surgical evaluation
evidenceTable.reference: Demetriades D, Salim A, Brown CV, et al. Lactate levels as predictors of bowel ischemia in patients with small bowel obstruction. Arch Surg. 2005;140(7):651-655.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Primary anastomosis after emergency bowel resection in the setting of obstruction is safe in haemodynamically stable patients with minimal contamination
evidenceTable.reference: Shih SP, Rogers AM, Sherwinter DA, et al. Emergency bowel resection and primary anastomosis for small bowel obstruction: a systematic review. J Gastrointest Surg. 2021;25(4):1052-1060.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Hartmann procedure remains the standard for emergency left-sided colonic obstruction when primary anastomosis is not safe
evidenceTable.reference: Ansaloni L, Andersson RE, Bazzoli F, et al. Guidelines in the management of obstructing cancer of the left colon: consensus conference of the WSES. World J Emerg Surg. 2010;5:29.

evidenceTable.recommendation: In patients with incarcerated hernia, gentle manual reduction under sedation may be attempted if no signs of strangulation are present
evidenceTable.reference: Sartelli M, Coccolini F, van Ramshorst GH, et al. WSES guidelines for emergency repair of complicated abdominal wall hernias. World J Emerg Surg. 2013;8(1):50.

evidenceTable.recommendation: Anti-adhesion barriers (such as hyaluronate-carboxymethylcellulose, icodextrin) may reduce the incidence and severity of recurrent adhesive SBO
evidenceTable.reference: ten Broek RP, Stommel MW, Strik C, et al. Benefits and harms of adhesion barriers for abdominal surgery: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Lancet. 2014;383(9911):48-59.

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm:
Suspected Bowel Obstruction
  │
  ├── History & Physical Exam
  │   ├── Colicky pain, vomiting, distension, obstipation
  │   ├── Check: previous scars, hernias (groin, umbilicus, incisional), DRE
  │   ├── Assoc: fever, tachycardia, constant pain → strangulation?
  │   └── Abdominal X-ray (erect + supine) IMMEDIATE
  │       ├── Air-fluid levels, dilated loops → obstruction confirmed
  │       │   └── Pattern: step-ladder (SBO) vs peripheral (LBO) vs coffee-bean (volvulus)
  │       ├── No air-fluid levels → ileus/pseudo-obstruction?
  │       └── Free air → perforation — emergency laparotomy
  ├── Laboratory
  │   ├── WBC, CRP, lactate, electrolytes, U+E, ABG
  │   └── Lactate >2 → suspect ischemia
  ├── CT Abdomen with IV contrast (GOLD STANDARD)
  │   ├── STRANGULATION SIGNS (surgery)
  │   │   ├── Closed-loop, mesenteric swirl, pneumatosis, portal venous gas
  │   │   └── Lactate >2 + CT signs → emergency laparotomy
  │   ├── TRANSITION POINT IDENTIFIED
  │   │   ├── Adhesive band → Gastrografin challenge (if no strangulation)
  │   │   ├── Incarcerated hernia → emergency repair
  │   │   ├── Colonic mass → CT staging → stent vs surgery
  │   │   ├── Volvulus → endoscopic detorsion (sigmoid) vs cecopexy (cecal)
  │   │   └── TB strictures → anti-TB therapy + elective resection
  │   └── NO TRANSITION POINT
  │       ├── Pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie's) → neostigmine, treat electrolytes
  │       ├── Adynamic ileus → treat underlying cause
  │       └── Severe constipation → disimpaction, enemas
  ├── Management Decision
  │   ├── Emergency surgery (within 2h): strangulation, perforation, necrotic hernia
  │   ├── Urgent surgery (within 12h): failed conservative 48h, complete LBO, irreducible hernia
  │   ├── Conservative trial: partial SBO, no strangulation, adhesive aetiology
  │   └── Gastrografin challenge: if contrast reaches colon in 6-24h → continue conservative; if not → surgery
  └── Special Populations
      ├── Children → consider intussusception, malrotation, hernia. US first.
      ├── Elderly → higher strangulation risk, lower threshold for CT/surgery
      ├── Post-laparotomy → adhesions most likely, but don't miss internal hernia
      └── HIV/TB → consider TB strictures, lymphoma, Kaposi sarcoma

managementPearls.context: The most important decision in bowel obstruction
managementPearls.pearl: The single most important decision is: simple or strangulated? A strangulated obstruction needs surgery within 2-3 hours; a simple obstruction can be managed conservatively for 24-48h. The key clues: pain that becomes constant (not colicky), fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, lactate >2, and CT findings of closed-loop or mesenteric edema. I tell my residents: 'When the pain stops going away between waves, start going to the OR.'
managementPearls.evidence: Demetriades D, Salim A, Brown CV, et al. Lactate levels as predictors of bowel ischemia in patients with small bowel obstruction. Arch Surg. 2005;140(7):651-655.

managementPearls.pearl: In every patient with bowel obstruction, examine the groin, umbilicus, and all surgical scars BEFORE doing any imaging. A strangulated femoral hernia in an elderly woman can present with minimal groin swelling (femoral canal is small) and no visible bulge — you MUST palpate the femoral canal with your index finger through the skin. I have personally found more than 10 obstructed hernias this way that were missed by junior doctors who went straight to CT. A CT will show it too, but you waste time.
managementPearls.evidence: Sartelli M, Coccolini F, van Ramshorst GH, et al. WSES guidelines for emergency repair of complicated abdominal wall hernias. World J Emerg Surg. 2013;8(1):50.

managementPearls.context: Gastrografin challenge — how to do it properly
managementPearls.pearl: The Gastrografin challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Give 100 mL of water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin or similar — NOT barium, which can cause peritonitis if it leaks) via NG tube, clamp for 1 hour, then take a supine abdominal X-ray at 4-6 hours. If contrast reaches the colon, the patient will almost certainly resolve without surgery (96% success). If not, they need surgery. The hyperosmolar contrast draws fluid into the bowel lumen, increasing hydrostatic pressure across the obstruction — this is the therapeutic mechanism.
managementPearls.evidence: Ceresoli M, Coccolini F, Catena F, et al. Water-soluble contrast agent in adhesive small bowel obstruction: a systematic review and meta-analysis. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:39.

managementPearls.context: When to operate on adhesive SBO in resource-limited settings
managementPearls.pearl: In our setting, the luxury of waiting 48h is not always available — patients often present late, and the risk of strangulation is higher. I use a modified approach: if the patient has a history of multiple previous laparotomies (suggesting dense adhesions), I operate earlier rather than later because the chance of resolution without surgery is lower. For first-time adhesive SBO, I wait 24h and do a Gastrografin challenge. If no contrast reaches the colon by 6h, I operate. This approach works well in our context.
managementPearls.evidence: Owuor G, Mutiso J, Opiyo A, et al. Management of adhesive small bowel obstruction in East Africa: a prospective multicenter study (AVOCADO). World J Surg. 2021;45(11):3302-3310.

managementPearls.pearl: When you open a patient who has had multiple previous laparotomies and you encounter dense adhesions that obliterate the peritoneal cavity, the safest approach is to decompress the NG tube, wait a few minutes, and look for the most dilated loop of bowel — it will be the most prominent and easiest to access. Start adhesiolysis there and work distally. If the plane between loops is completely obliterated, it is safer to do a limited adhesiolysis to relieve the obstruction and accept that you may not resolve all adhesions, rather than risk multiple enterotomies. Never try to 'separate every loop' — that is how you create fistulas.
managementPearls.evidence: ten Broek RP, Strik C, Issa Y, et al. Adhesiolysis-related morbidity in abdominal surgery. Ann Surg. 2013;258(1):98-106.

managementPearls.pearl: The classic criteria for bowel viability are colour, peristalsis, and mesenteric pulsations. But these can be misleading. The most reliable method is: wrap the bowel in warm saline-soaked packs for 10 minutes and reassess. If colour returns to pink, peristalsis is visible, and the mesenteric edge bleeds when cut with a scalpel, the bowel is viable. If still grey/black, it must be resected. If you are uncertain, resect — it is far better to remove 10 cm of viable bowel than to leave 1 cm of non-viable bowel and have a leak. Use a Doppler probe if available.
managementPearls.evidence: Urbanavicius L, Pattyn P, Van de Putte D, Venskutonis D. How to assess intestinal viability during surgery: a review of techniques. World J Gastrointest Surg. 2011;3(5):59-69.

managementPearls.context: Stoma decision in emergency bowel resection
managementPearls.pearl: The decision to create a stoma vs perform primary anastomosis is one of the most critical intraoperative decisions. My rule: if the patient has more than 2 of the following — haemodynamic instability, preoperative sepsis, fecal contamination, severe malnutrition (albumin <25), or steroid use — I create a stoma. A temporary ileostomy is better than a leaking anastomosis that kills the patient. I tell patients pre-operatively: 'There is a possibility you may wake up with a bag on your belly.' This consent is critical.
managementPearls.evidence: Shih SP, Rogers AM, Sherwinter DA, et al. Emergency bowel resection and primary anastomosis for small bowel obstruction. J Gastrointest Surg. 2021;25(4):1052-1060.

managementPearls.context: Gallstone ileus — a trap for the unwary
managementPearls.pearl: Gallstone ileus accounts for only 1-3% of SBO but carries 15-20% mortality because it typically affects elderly women with comorbidities. The classic Rigler triad (pneumobilia, SBO, ectopic stone) on CT is diagnostic. The stone usually lodges at the terminal ileum (narrowest point of small bowel). At surgery, make an enterotomy proximal to the stone, extract it, and carefully run the entire small bowel looking for a second stone (present in 5-10%). The debate about one-stage vs two-stage surgery persists, but I prefer enterolithotomy alone in the emergency setting and defer cholecystectomy + fistula repair to an elective procedure.
managementPearls.evidence: Mallipeddi MK, Pappas TN, Shapiro ML, et al. Gallstone ileus: revisiting surgical outcomes using National Surgical Quality Improvement Program data. J Surg Res. 2018;228:1-5.

managementPearls.pearl: After laparotomy for SBO, it can be difficult to distinguish between post-operative ileus (expected) and ongoing obstruction (needs reoperation). If the patient has high NG output (>1 L/day) beyond day 3-4, has persistent distension, and no flatus by day 5, suspect ongoing obstruction. A CT with oral contrast can help: if contrast passes through the entire bowel to the colon, it is ileus; if it stops at a point, it is ongoing mechanical obstruction. Do a Gastrografin challenge before rushing back to the OR.
managementPearls.evidence: Vather R, O'Grady G, Bissett IP, Dinning PG. Postoperative ileus: mechanisms and future directions for research. Clin Exp Pharmacol Physiol. 2014;41(5):358-370.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Entering the peritoneal cavity through a previous midline scar — the underlying bowel is often densely adherent to the posterior rectus sheath and can be injured before you visualize it
operativeNuances.expertTip: Extend the incision above and below the previous scar to enter the peritoneal cavity through virgin territory (e.g., above the xiphisternum or below the previous Pfannenstiel). Once in the peritoneal cavity, place your finger and gently palpate the adhesions to the previous scar from inside, then divide them under direct vision from inside-out. This 'inside-out' approach is much safer than the 'outside-in' approach. If the previous scar is a midline, go in at the upper end — the falciform ligament often protects the bowel there.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Bowel injury during Veress needle or first trocar insertion in a distended abdomen with dilated loops of bowel
operativeNuances.expertTip: NEVER use Veress needle in SBO with distension — the dilated bowel loops are directly beneath the abdominal wall. Use open (Hasson) technique at Palmer's point (left subcostal, midclavicular line) which is usually free of adhesions. Make a 2 cm incision, dissect down to the fascia under direct vision, incise fascia and peritoneum, and place the port under direct finger guidance. This is slower but safer. Once the camera is in, look at the entry site for any bowel injury before proceeding.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Performing an anastomosis in ischemic bowel ends that appear viable at first assessment but become necrotic post-operatively
operativeNuances.expertTip: When you have resected the obviously non-viable bowel, do NOT perform the anastomosis at the first operation. Staple both ends and leave them in discontinuity. Mark them with long silk stay sutures for easy identification. Plan a second-look laparotomy in 24-48 hours. At the second look, the demarcation between viable and non-viable bowel will be clear, and you can safely perform your anastomosis. This 'damage control' approach has saved many patients from anastomotic leaks.

operativeNuances.procedure: Hartmann Procedure for Left Colon Obstruction
operativeNuances.pitfall: Inadequate length of the descending colon to reach the abdominal wall as an end colostomy after sigmoid resection
operativeNuances.expertTip: Before committing to the Hartmann, ensure the descending colon is long enough to reach the abdominal wall without tension. Mobilize the splenic flexure by dividing the lateral peritoneal reflection of the descending colon (white line of Toldt) and the phrenicocolic ligament. If still inadequate, consider dividing the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin from the aorta (high ligation) — this provides additional length by allowing the colon to be brought up from its mesenteric tether. Mark the stoma site pre-operatively if possible.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Reducing an incarcerated hernia 'en masse' — pushing the entire hernia sac including the constricting neck into the abdomen, which relieves the external swelling but leaves the bowel still obstructed and potentially strangulated
operativeNuances.expertTip: Never attempt taxis (manual reduction) of an incarcerated hernia if there are signs of strangulation (tenderness, erythema, fever, tachycardia). If you attempt taxis, use the five 'Ps' — Position (Trendelenburg), Pain control (sedation), Patience (slow sustained pressure), Palpation (grasp the neck and reduce the contents first, not the sac), and Post-reduction (observe for 24h). The technique: apply gentle, steady pressure on the hernia mass directed towards the external ring, using your thumb and index finger to guide the contents through the canal. If the hernia reduces with a 'gurgle', the reduction is successful. If the patient develops worsening pain or peritonitis, operate immediately.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Attempting endoscopic detorsion when the sigmoid colon is already gangrenous — the procedure is not only futile but dangerous, as insufflation can perforate the necrotic bowel
operativeNuances.expertTip: Before attempting flexible sigmoidoscopy or rigid proctoscopy for detorsion, check for signs of gangrene: peritoneal signs, fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, and CT findings of pneumatosis or non-enhancing bowel wall. If ANY of these are present, go straight to laparotomy. When performing detorsion, use gentle insufflation — just enough to visualize the lumen. Pass a well-lubricated rectal tube (size 24-30 Fr) through the scope past the volvulus twist. The tube should be sutured to the perianal skin and left in place for 48-72h to maintain decompression and prevent early recurrence.

prognosis.overallMortality: Overall mortality for mechanical bowel obstruction is 3-10%. For simple adhesive SBO managed conservatively: <1%. For strangulated SBO requiring resection: 8-15%. For colonic obstruction with perforation: 15-25%. For patients with septic shock and multi-organ failure: 30-50%. In East Africa, overall mortality is higher (8-15%) due to delayed presentation and limited ICU capacity.
prognosis.morbidity: Wound infection: 10-15% for emergency laparotomy, higher with stoma or contamination. Anastomotic leak: 3-5% for primary anastomosis in emergency setting. Intra-abdominal abscess: 5-10%. Short bowel syndrome: 1-2% after massive resection. Incisional hernia: 10-15% within 5 years. Stoma complications: 20-30% of patients with emergency stoma (parastomal hernia, retraction, prolapse, skin breakdown).
prognosis.recurrence: Recurrent adhesive SBO: 15-20% within 5 years after conservative management; 10-15% after adhesiolysis. Anti-adhesion barriers may reduce recurrence by 30%. Recurrence rate is higher in patients with multiple prior laparotomies or a history of previous SBO episodes. Sigmoid volvulus: 40-60% recurrence after endoscopic detorsion alone; 5-10% after sigmoid resection. Incarcerated hernia: <5% recurrence after mesh repair, 10-15% after tissue repair.
prognosis.survivalData: Five-year survival after successful management of simple SBO is >90% (depends on underlying cause — malignancy reduces survival). For strangulated SBO requiring resection, 1-year survival is 80-85%. For patients developing short bowel syndrome, 5-year survival is 60-70% depending on remnant bowel length and quality of nutritional support. Patients surviving Hartmann procedure for malignant obstruction: 5-year survival 40-50% (stage-dependent).
prognosis.landmarkReference: ten Broek RP, Issa Y, van Santbrink EJ, et al. Burden of adhesions in abdominal and pelvic surgery: systematic review and met-analysis. BMJ. 2013;347:f5588.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Simple/Partial (NON-SEVERE) vs Complete/Strangulated (SEVERE) — peritonitis, closed-loop, or ischemia = SEVERE
